questions.passage:
In the late eighteenth century a new word entered the vocabulary of taste and quickly grew tyrannical. The picturesque named a quality of landscape prized for being, quite literally, like a picture, and its champions taught a generation of travelers how to look at scenery as though composing it. Between the beautiful, which pleased by smoothness and gentle regularity, and the sublime, which overwhelmed by vastness and terror, the picturesque staked out a middle ground defined by roughness, irregularity, and variety, the ruined tower preferred to the sound one, the gnarled oak to the shapely, the winding lane to the straight road.

The apostles of the picturesque did not merely describe this taste; they codified it into a discipline. Travelers were instructed where to stand, what to include and exclude, how to frame a valley so that a dark mass of foreground trees might set off the luminous distance. Some carried a tinted glass that reduced a living prospect to the muted tones of an old master's canvas, turning their backs on the actual view in order to admire its reflection. There is something faintly comic in the spectacle, the tourist who has traveled to a famous scene and then contrives not to look at it directly, but the impulse behind it was serious. It was an attempt to teach the untrained eye how to see, to convert a vague pleasure in scenery into a considered and communicable judgment.

Whether the discipline improved perception or corrupted it is a fair question, and the answer is not one-sided. On the credit side, the picturesque gave people a vocabulary and a set of principles where before they had only inarticulate reactions. It drew attention to composition, to the play of light and shadow, to the value of contrast and the interest of the irregular, and it sent thousands out to look at their own countryside with a new and genuine attentiveness. A person who has learned to notice how a foreground frames a distance is, in a real sense, seeing more than before.

On the debit side, the same discipline could substitute a formula for the very attentiveness it promised. The traveler armed with rules for what made a proper prospect risked seeing only whether a given scene obeyed them, praising a landscape for resembling a painting rather than for being itself. Roughness was prescribed so insistently that a tidy, prosperous farm could be dismissed as unpaintable, and real places were judged by how well they imitated the conventions of a studio. The category that opened the eye could, in the same motion, narrow it, teaching people to want from a landscape only what a picture had taught them to expect.

The deeper irony is that the picturesque prized wildness and irregularity by the most artificial of means. It admired the unplanned and the accidental, yet reached them through an elaborate apparatus of rules, framing devices, and borrowed conventions. The taste for the rough was itself highly cultivated; the love of the natural was mediated at every step by art. This is not a contradiction that discredits the picturesque so much as one that defines it, and that it shares with much of aesthetic education, which always risks replacing the thing seen with the manner of seeing it.

What the episode illustrates, then, is a permanent hazard of any trained perception. To learn to see is to acquire categories, and categories both reveal and conceal. The picturesque revealed a great deal that casual looking had missed, and concealed whatever its formula had no place for. It deserves neither the ridicule its excesses invite nor the reverence its advocates demanded, but rather the qualified credit due to any tool that sharpens sight in one direction at the cost of dulling it in another.
questions.stem: The author's overall assessment of the picturesque is that it

choices.A: both sharpened perception and risked narrowing it, deserving qualified rather than total credit
choices.B: wholly corrupted the taste of those who adopted its rules
choices.C: was a very nearly flawless method for training the untrained eye that its harshest critics quite unfairly mocked
choices.D: had no lasting effect on how people looked at landscape

explanations.A: Correct. The author weighs a credit side, a new attentiveness to composition and light, against a debit side, the substitution of a formula for attentiveness, and concludes the picturesque deserves the qualified credit due to any tool that sharpens sight in one direction while dulling it in another.
explanations.B: The passage grants real gains, that people learned to notice composition and saw their countryside anew, so wholesale corruption misstates the balanced verdict.
explanations.C: The author details genuine faults, praising landscapes only for resembling paintings, so the method is not treated as flawless.
explanations.D: The passage credits the picturesque with sending thousands to look at scenery with new attentiveness, so it plainly had an effect.

questions.stem: The picturesque is defined in the passage by contrast with the beautiful and the sublime as the aesthetic quality marked by

choices.A: mathematical proportion inherited from classical architecture
choices.B: smoothness and gentle regularity that please without exciting
choices.C: vastness and terror that overwhelm the beholder
choices.D: roughness, irregularity, and variety occupying a middle ground between them

explanations.A: Classical proportion is never invoked; the picturesque is characterized by the irregular and rough, the opposite of strict mathematical order.
explanations.B: Smoothness and gentle regularity are the author's description of the beautiful, the category the picturesque is set against, not of the picturesque itself.
explanations.C: Vastness and terror define the sublime in the passage; the picturesque is expressly distinguished from that overwhelming quality.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage places the picturesque between the beautiful and the sublime, defined by roughness, irregularity, and variety, favoring the ruined tower over the sound one and the gnarled oak over the shapely.

questions.stem: The traveler who uses a tinted glass to view a landscape is presented as an example of

choices.A: a broadly scientific method for objectively measuring the true colors that were present in a distant scene
choices.B: the impulse to see nature through the mediation of art, even at the cost of the direct view
choices.C: a rejection of the picturesque in favor of unmediated looking
choices.D: the superiority of memory over direct observation

explanations.A: The tinted glass reduces the view to painterly tones, an aesthetic filter, not a scientific instrument for measuring true color.
explanations.B: Correct. The tourist who turns from the living prospect to admire its muted reflection dramatizes the passage's theme that the love of the natural was mediated at every step by art, reaching the scene through the conventions of an old master's canvas.
explanations.C: The device embodies the picturesque discipline rather than rejecting it; it is the extreme of seeing nature as a picture, not a return to direct looking.
explanations.D: The example concerns mediated versus direct seeing of a present scene, not any comparison between memory and observation.

questions.stem: The passage suggests that a tidy, prosperous farm would be dismissed by strict devotees of the picturesque because it

choices.A: displayed the vastness proper to the sublime
choices.B: was too remote for travelers to reach conveniently
choices.C: lacked the roughness and irregularity their formula demanded
choices.D: resembled an old master's painting too closely

explanations.A: Vastness belongs to the sublime, not to the picturesque standard; the farm is rejected for insufficient roughness, not for grandeur.
explanations.B: Remoteness is never mentioned as a criterion; the farm is faulted for being tidy and regular, not for being hard to reach.
explanations.C: Correct. The author writes that roughness was prescribed so insistently that a tidy, prosperous farm could be dismissed as unpaintable, judged against studio conventions rather than on its own terms. Its smooth order failed the formula.
explanations.D: The devotees prized resemblance to paintings; a farm dismissed as unpaintable fails precisely because it does not resemble the preferred pictures.

questions.stem: The author calls it 'the deeper irony' that the picturesque

choices.A: ultimately came to value smoothness and gentle regularity despite loudly claiming to prize roughness above all
choices.B: was invented by painters who never traveled to see real landscapes
choices.C: was more concerned with architecture than with natural scenery
choices.D: reached its love of the wild and accidental through an elaborate apparatus of artificial rules

explanations.A: The picturesque prized roughness, not smoothness; the irony is that it pursued roughness artificially, not that it secretly preferred the smooth.
explanations.B: The irony concerns the artificial means used to reach a taste for the natural, not the travels or lack of travels of painters.
explanations.C: The picturesque in this passage concerns landscape scenery; architecture is not its primary object, so this misidentifies the irony.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage identifies the irony that the picturesque admired the unplanned and accidental yet reached them through rules, framing devices, and borrowed conventions, so the taste for the rough was itself highly cultivated and the love of the natural mediated by art.

questions.stem: Which situation would the author most likely regard as parallel to the case of the picturesque?

choices.A: A traveler who refuses every guidebook and convention alike and therefore forms no considered opinion about the view at all
choices.B: A wine course that opens a novice to new flavors yet trains him to scorn any bottle falling outside its approved styles
choices.C: A painter who discards every inherited convention and technique and works from no method or formal training at all
choices.D: A surveyor who measures a landscape's exact dimensions with complete and wholly detached scientific objectivity

explanations.A: The traveler who forms no opinions has acquired no categories at all, so she illustrates neither the gain nor the loss the picturesque involves.
explanations.B: Correct. The author draws the general moral that learning to see acquires categories that both reveal and conceal, sharpening sight in one direction at the cost of another. A wine course that opens the palate to new flavors while narrowing it to approved styles mirrors that double effect exactly.
explanations.C: A painter working with no technique abandons the trained perception the passage discusses, missing the point that categories both help and hinder.
explanations.D: Objective measurement is not the kind of trained aesthetic perception at issue; the parallel the author draws concerns categories that shape taste, not neutral quantification.
